quiz_questions.question_text: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygous phenotype is:

options.text: Identical to the dominant homozygous phenotype
An intermediate blend between the two homozygous phenotypes
Identical to the recessive homozygous phenotype
A completely new phenotype unrelated to either parent

quiz_questions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, neither allele is fully dominant, so the heterozygote shows a phenotype intermediate between the two homozygotes. A classic example is red x white snapdragons producing pink heterozygotes.

options.text: One allele completely masks the other
Both alleles are fully and simultaneously expressed in the heterozygote
The heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype
A single gene controls multiple traits

quiz_questions.explanation: In codominance, both alleles are expressed equally and simultaneously in the heterozygote, rather than blending. A classic example is ABO blood type, where the I^A and I^B alleles are codominant, producing the AB blood type.

quiz_questions.question_text: A gene that has three or more alleles in the population is an example of:

options.text: Incomplete dominance
Multiple alleles
Polygenic inheritance
Sex-linked inheritance

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiple alleles means that a single gene has more than two allelic forms in the population, although any individual still carries at most two alleles. The ABO blood group gene (I^A, I^B, i) is a classic example with three alleles.

quiz_questions.question_text: X-linked recessive traits appear more frequently in males because:

options.text: Males have two X chromosomes, doubling the chance of expression
Males have only one X chromosome, so a single recessive allele on the X will be expressed with no dominant allele to mask it
The Y chromosome always enhances recessive allele expression
Females cannot carry X-linked alleles

quiz_questions.explanation: Males are hemizygous for X-linked genes (XY), so they have only one copy. A recessive allele on the X has no corresponding allele on the Y to mask it, meaning the trait is always expressed. Females (XX) need two copies of the recessive allele for expression.

options.text: A single gene controlling multiple traits
Two or more genes contributing to a single phenotypic trait, producing continuous variation
One gene with multiple alleles
Genes inherited from only one parent

quiz_questions.explanation: Polygenic inheritance involves multiple genes (each with a small additive effect) contributing to a single trait. This produces a continuous range of phenotypes (bell curve distribution). Human skin color, height, and intelligence are polygenic traits.

options.text: Multiple genes control one trait
One gene influences multiple, seemingly unrelated phenotypic traits
An allele is codominant with another allele
Genes are linked on the same chromosome

quiz_questions.explanation: Pleiotropy is when a single gene affects multiple phenotypic traits. For example, the sickle cell allele (HbS) affects red blood cell shape, oxygen transport, spleen function, and resistance to malaria.

options.text: One gene masks or modifies the expression of another gene at a different locus
Two alleles of the same gene interact to produce a blended phenotype
A gene is expressed only when the organism is homozygous
Environmental factors override genetic effects

quiz_questions.explanation: Epistasis occurs when the product of one gene masks or modifies the phenotypic expression of another gene. In Labrador coat color, the E gene is epistatic to the B gene: ee genotype results in yellow coat regardless of the B gene genotype.

options.text: Traits passed equally from both parents
Traits inherited exclusively from the mother through mitochondrial DNA
Traits that only affect males
Traits encoded on the Y chromosome

quiz_questions.explanation: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited exclusively from the mother because the egg contributes virtually all the cytoplasm (and mitochondria) to the zygote. Traits encoded by mtDNA show maternal inheritance, affecting all offspring of an affected mother.

options.text: Both parental alleles being expressed equally
The expression of a gene depending on which parent it was inherited from, due to epigenetic silencing of one allele
Genes being physically removed from one parental chromosome
Identical phenotypes in all offspring regardless of genotype

quiz_questions.explanation: In genomic imprinting, certain genes are epigenetically marked (typically by DNA methylation) during gamete formation so that only the allele from one parent is expressed. The other parent's allele is silenced. Prader-Willi and Angelman syndromes are examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cross between a red-flowered (RR) and white-flowered (WW) snapdragon produces pink-flowered (RW) offspring. When two pink-flowered plants are crossed, the expected phenotypic ratio is:

options.text: 3 red : 1 white
1 red : 2 pink : 1 white
All pink
1 red : 1 pink : 1 white

quiz_questions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, the phenotypic ratio matches the genotypic ratio. RW x RW produces 1 RR (red) : 2 RW (pink) : 1 WW (white), a 1:2:1 phenotypic ratio instead of the typical 3:1 of complete dominance.

options.text: A pattern of inheritance where the dominant allele completely masks the recessive allele
A pattern of inheritance where the heterozygote exhibits an intermediate phenotype between those of the two homozygotes because neither allele is fully dominant
A pattern where both alleles are simultaneously and fully expressed
A pattern where gene expression depends on the environment

quiz_questions.explanation: Incomplete dominance is a non-Mendelian inheritance pattern where the heterozygous phenotype is an intermediate blend of the two homozygous phenotypes, because the single copy of the dominant allele does not produce enough gene product for full expression.

options.text: An inheritance pattern where both alleles in a heterozygote are fully and independently expressed, so the phenotype shows both traits simultaneously rather than a blend
An inheritance pattern where the dominant allele prevents any expression of the recessive allele
A situation where two different genes control the same trait
An inheritance pattern where the heterozygote has a phenotype intermediate between the homozygotes

quiz_questions.explanation: Codominance means both alleles are expressed simultaneously and fully in the heterozygote. Unlike incomplete dominance (blending), codominant traits show both phenotypes distinctly. Human AB blood type (showing both A and B antigens) is the classic example.

options.text: A type of interaction between genes at different loci where one gene masks or modifies the phenotypic expression of another gene
The interaction between two alleles of the same gene
The phenomenon where crossing over occurs between two genes
The enhancement of gene expression by environmental factors

quiz_questions.explanation: Epistasis is the interaction between genes at different loci, where the expression of one gene (the epistatic gene) modifies or suppresses the phenotypic expression of another gene (the hypostatic gene). It alters expected Mendelian ratios.

options.text: The condition where multiple genes affect a single phenotypic trait
The phenomenon where a single gene influences multiple, often seemingly unrelated, phenotypic traits
The inheritance of genes from both parents equally
The tendency for linked genes to be inherited together

quiz_questions.explanation: Pleiotropy occurs when one gene has effects on multiple phenotypic traits. This happens because a gene's protein product may function in different tissues or pathways. Marfan syndrome, caused by a single fibrillin gene mutation, affects the eyes, heart, and skeleton.

options.text: Inheritance of traits controlled by a single gene with multiple alleles
Inheritance of traits determined by the additive effects of two or more genes, resulting in continuous phenotypic variation
Inheritance of traits linked to the X chromosome
Inheritance through mitochondrial DNA

quiz_questions.explanation: Polygenic inheritance involves multiple genes, each contributing a small amount to a single trait. The combined effects produce a continuous distribution of phenotypes (bell curve). Human height, skin color, and eye color are polygenic traits.

options.text: A gene located on an autosome near a sex-determining region
A gene located on the X chromosome, resulting in sex-specific inheritance patterns
A gene that is only expressed during the development of sex organs
A gene that crosses over between the X and Y chromosomes

quiz_questions.explanation: X-linked genes are located on the X chromosome. Because males (XY) have only one X, they express any recessive allele present. Females (XX) need two copies of a recessive allele for expression, making them carriers if heterozygous.

options.text: The physical stamping of chromosomes during cell division
An epigenetic phenomenon where specific genes are expressed in a parent-of-origin-specific manner, with one parental allele silenced by methylation
The copying of DNA sequences from one chromosome to another
The random inactivation of one X chromosome in females

quiz_questions.explanation: Genomic imprinting is an epigenetic process where chemical modifications (usually DNA methylation) silence one allele based on its parental origin. Only the allele from one specific parent is expressed, making the gene functionally hemizygous.

options.text: Inheritance of genes located on the X chromosome from the mother
The inheritance of organellar DNA (mitochondria and chloroplasts) exclusively from the mother, since the egg provides the cytoplasm to the zygote
The preferential expression of the mother's autosomal genes
A pattern where only daughters inherit traits from the mother

quiz_questions.explanation: Maternal inheritance describes traits encoded by mitochondrial (or chloroplast) DNA, which is inherited exclusively from the mother. The egg cell contributes virtually all the cytoplasm and its organelles to the embryo, while sperm contribute almost none.

options.text: The deletion of one X chromosome in females during embryonic development
The random transcriptional silencing of one X chromosome in each cell of female mammals to achieve dosage compensation between XX females and XY males
The inactivation of all sex-linked genes in males
The mutation of genes on the X chromosome that makes them nonfunctional

quiz_questions.explanation: X-inactivation (lyonization) is the random silencing of one X chromosome in each somatic cell of female mammals. This occurs early in embryonic development, and the same X remains inactive in all descendant cells, creating a mosaic pattern of X-linked gene expression.

options.text: Inheritance of genes located on the sex chromosomes
Inheritance of autosomal genes whose expression is limited to one sex due to hormonal or anatomical differences, even though both sexes carry the gene
Traits that can only be passed from father to son
Inheritance patterns exclusive to organisms that reproduce asexually

quiz_questions.explanation: Sex-limited traits are encoded by autosomal genes but expressed in only one sex due to hormonal regulation. For example, male pattern baldness and milk production are influenced by sex hormones, so the trait appears only (or predominantly) in one sex.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple has a child with type AB blood. The mother has type A blood and the father has type B blood. Based on the ABO blood group system, what are the most likely genotypes of the parents?

options.text: Mother I^A I^A, Father I^B I^B
Mother I^A i, Father I^B i
Mother I^A I^B, Father ii
Mother ii, Father I^A I^B

quiz_questions.explanation: For a child to have type AB blood (I^A I^B), they must receive I^A from one parent and I^B from the other. The type A mother could be I^A I^A or I^A i, and the type B father could be I^B I^B or I^B i. However, since the question asks for the most likely genotypes (and both could also have a type O child if heterozygous), I^A i and I^B i are the most likely.

quiz_questions.question_text: A woman who is a carrier for color blindness (X^C X^c) marries a man with normal color vision (X^C Y). What percentage of their sons would be expected to be color blind?
quiz_questions.explanation: Sons receive their X chromosome from their mother. The carrier mother (X^C X^c) has a 50% chance of passing the X^c allele to each son. Sons who receive X^c Y will be color blind (50%), while sons who receive X^C Y will have normal vision (50%).

quiz_questions.question_text: Calico cats display patches of orange and black fur, with the orange gene (O) located on the X chromosome. Male calico cats are extremely rare. Why are most calico cats female?

options.text: The calico pattern requires two X chromosomes, one carrying each allele (X^O X^o), and random X-inactivation creates patches of each color
Males cannot carry the orange allele
The calico gene is autosomal but expressed only in females due to hormones
Calico is caused by a mitochondrial gene inherited only from the mother

quiz_questions.explanation: Calico coloring requires heterozygosity at the X-linked orange gene (X^O X^o). Random X-inactivation in each cell produces a mosaic pattern of orange and black patches. Males (XY) have only one X, so they express only one color (unless they have an XXY genotype).

quiz_questions.question_text: In Labrador retrievers, coat color depends on two genes: the B gene (B = black, b = brown) and the E gene (E = color deposited, e = no color deposited). A BbEe x BbEe cross produces puppies in a 9:3:4 ratio (9 black : 3 brown : 4 yellow). What type of gene interaction is occurring?

options.text: Codominance between the B and E genes
Recessive epistasis, where the ee genotype at the E locus masks the expression of the B gene
Incomplete dominance at both loci
Polygenic inheritance of coat color

quiz_questions.explanation: This is recessive epistasis: the ee genotype (homozygous recessive at the E locus) prevents any pigment from being deposited in the coat, producing a yellow dog regardless of the B genotype. The 9:3:4 ratio is a modification of the 9:3:3:1 ratio where the last two classes (3+1=4) are combined.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sickle cell disease is often described as a pleiotropic condition. A student asks why a single mutation in hemoglobin causes so many different symptoms (anemia, organ damage, pain crises, malaria resistance). What is the best explanation?

options.text: Each symptom is caused by a different mutation in a different gene
The single hemoglobin mutation causes red blood cells to sickle, which then affects circulation, oxygen delivery, and organ function throughout the body
Sickle cell disease involves mutations in multiple genes that happen to be linked
The symptoms are caused by environmental factors rather than the mutation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is pleiotropy: one gene (HBB) with one mutation (glutamic acid to valine) produces abnormal hemoglobin that causes red blood cells to sickle under low oxygen. The sickled cells then cause a cascade of effects in multiple organ systems, producing the diverse symptoms of sickle cell disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: Human skin color is controlled by at least three genes, each with two alleles that contribute additively. A person with genotype AABBCC (darkest) crosses with a person with genotype aabbcc (lightest). What skin color would you expect in the F1 offspring?

options.text: All F1 offspring would have the darkest skin color
All F1 offspring would have intermediate skin color (AaBbCc, with 3 of 6 possible contributing alleles)
F1 offspring would show a range of skin colors from lightest to darkest
All F1 offspring would have the lightest skin color

quiz_questions.explanation: In polygenic inheritance with additive alleles, the F1 generation from AABBCC x aabbcc would all be AaBbCc, with exactly 3 'contributing' alleles out of 6 possible. This produces a uniform intermediate phenotype in the F1 generation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A genetic disorder is traced through a family and shows the following pattern: all children of an affected mother are affected, but an affected father never passes the trait to his children. What type of inheritance is this?

options.text: Autosomal dominant
X-linked recessive
Mitochondrial (maternal) inheritance
Autosomal recessive

quiz_questions.explanation: This is the hallmark of mitochondrial inheritance: affected mothers pass the trait to all their children, while affected fathers pass it to none. This is because mitochondria (and their DNA) come exclusively from the egg, not from sperm.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two parents who both have intermediate height have children who range from very short to very tall. This wide range of offspring phenotypes from intermediate parents is best explained by:

options.text: Autosomal recessive inheritance
Polygenic inheritance, where many genes contribute additively and recombination produces offspring with different numbers of contributing alleles
X-linked inheritance of the height gene
Incomplete dominance at a single gene locus

quiz_questions.explanation: Height is polygenic. When intermediate-height parents (heterozygous at multiple loci) cross, independent assortment produces offspring with many possible combinations of alleles, generating a continuous range from short to tall in the F2-like generation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Prader-Willi syndrome and Angelman syndrome are both caused by deletions of the same region on chromosome 15, yet they produce very different symptoms. The key difference is which parent the deletion is inherited from. What genetic mechanism explains this?

options.text: X-linked inheritance
Genomic imprinting, where genes in this region are differentially silenced depending on parental origin
Mitochondrial inheritance
Polygenic inheritance with environmental interaction

quiz_questions.explanation: Genes in the chromosome 15q11-13 region are imprinted: some are only expressed from the paternal copy and others only from the maternal copy. Deletion of the paternal copy causes Prader-Willi syndrome; deletion of the maternal copy causes Angelman syndrome.

quiz_questions.question_text: A breeder crosses two chickens: one with white feathers and one with black feathers. All F1 offspring have blue (grayish) feathers. When the F1 are crossed with each other, the F2 generation shows 1/4 white, 1/2 blue, and 1/4 black. However, examining the blue feathers closely reveals that they contain both white and black pigment granules side by side. Is this incomplete dominance or codominance?

options.text: Incomplete dominance, because the phenotype appears intermediate
Codominance, because both white and black pigments are individually expressed in separate granules rather than being blended
Complete dominance with variable expressivity
Epistasis between a pigment gene and a modifier gene

quiz_questions.explanation: Although the birds appear 'blue' from a distance, the presence of both distinct white and black pigment granules indicates codominance: both alleles are independently and fully expressed at the cellular level. In true incomplete dominance, a single intermediate pigment would be produced.
